label: Zhongguancun Science Park, Beijing
sub: China's 'Silicon Valley' — home to Baidu, Lenovo, ByteDance, and 10,000+ tech companies. Hosts top AI research universities including Peking and Tsinghua. $200B+ annual tech output.

label: Huawei HQ, Shenzhen
sub: Huawei's 1.5 million sqm global headquarters campus in Bantian district, Shenzhen. The company employs 207,000 globally, with 45% in R&D. Shenzhen is also home to BYD, DJI, OnePlus, and Tencent.

label: Alibaba HQ, Hangzhou
sub: Alibaba's global headquarters where Jack Ma founded the company in 1999. Alibaba Cloud, Ant Group, and AI research labs are based here. The 2021 regulatory crackdown resulting in a $2.75B fine originated from SAMR investigations.

label: Shanghai Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park
sub: China's largest semiconductor cluster outside of Beijing. Home to SMIC Shanghai fabs, NIO EV R&D, Meituan logistics AI, and dozens of chip design companies. Key node in China's integrated circuit supply chain.

label: ByteDance HQ, Beijing
sub: Headquarters of ByteDance, the world's most valuable private tech company at $440B+. Creator of TikTok (Douyin), Toutiao, and Lark. ByteDance's $155B 2024 revenue makes it larger than Netflix and Airbnb combined.

label: BYD HQ, Shenzhen
sub: Headquarters of BYD, the world's largest EV manufacturer by units sold. BYD surpassed Tesla in global EV sales in Q4 2023. Integrates battery, semiconductor, and vehicle manufacturing — a model of China's vertical tech integration strategy.

label: DJI HQ, Shenzhen
sub: DJI controls 70-80% of the global consumer drone market. Added to US Entity List in 2020 for alleged military connections. DJI's Phantom and Mavic drones have dominated civilian and commercial markets, demonstrating China's ability to dominate a technology-intensive hardware sector.

label: YMTC Fab, Wuhan
sub: Yangtze Memory Technologies Corporation's primary NAND flash memory manufacturing facility in Wuhan's Optics Valley. Added to US Entity List in December 2022. YMTC achieved 232-layer 3D NAND and produces 500,000 domestic wafers per month.

label: SMIC HQ & Fab, Shanghai
sub: Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation's headquarters and primary fab in Zhangjiang. Added to US Entity List December 2020. SMIC achieved a 7nm-equivalent (N+2) process for Huawei's Kirin 9000S chip without EUV lithography — a major technical milestone under sanctions.

label: CXMT DRAM Fab, Hefei
sub: ChangXin Memory Technologies (长鑫存储) — China's primary DRAM manufacturer, producing DDR4 memory chips in Hefei. CXMT has reached 17nm DRAM technology, closing the gap with Samsung and SK Hynix. Backed by Hefei city government and national semiconductor funds.

label: SMIC North Fab, Beijing
sub: SMIC's northern manufacturing facility in Beijing, expanding capacity for 28nm and legacy process nodes. China has invested heavily in expanding legacy-node capacity to serve the automotive, industrial, and IoT sectors where advanced chips are not required.

label: TSMC Nanjing Fab
sub: TSMC's 16nm fab in Nanjing — TSMC's only facility in mainland China. Under US pressure, TSMC has stopped advanced technology transfers to Nanjing and is prohibited from expanding beyond existing capacity. Remains a source of geopolitical sensitivity.

label: TSMC HQ & 3nm Fabs, Hsinchu
sub: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's headquarters and most advanced fabs in Hsinchu Science Park. TSMC produces over 90% of the world's advanced chips (below 7nm). The single most strategically important tech facility globally — often called 'Silicon Shield' protecting Taiwan from Chinese military action.

label: MediaTek HQ, Hsinchu
sub: MediaTek is the world's largest mobile chip designer by units shipped, powering budget and mid-range smartphones globally. MediaTek chips are critical to China's domestic handset industry. The company navigates complex US-Taiwan-China trade dynamics as it serves both Chinese OEMs and maintains US customer relationships.

label: Intel Assembly & Test, Chengdu
sub: Intel's major assembly, test, and packaging facility in Chengdu — one of Intel's largest facilities globally. The facility employs thousands and packages Intel processors for global distribution. Intel's investment in China illustrates the tension between decoupling goals and existing multinational footprints.

label: Samsung NAND Fab, Xi'an
sub: Samsung's NAND flash memory manufacturing facility in Xi'an — Samsung's largest single semiconductor investment globally. Produces ~40% of Samsung's total NAND output. Samsung has navigated US export controls by receiving temporary waivers allowing continued operation despite being a US ally.

label: Huawei HiSilicon Chip Design, Suzhou
sub: Huawei's HiSilicon semiconductor subsidiary maintains major chip design centers across China including Suzhou. HiSilicon designed the Kirin 9000S chip manufactured by SMIC. As of 2023, HiSilicon employs 7,000+ engineers and is accelerating AI chip design for Ascend series accelerators.

label: Guangzhou AI & Gaming Tech Hub
sub: Guangzhou hosts major offices for NetEase, Tencent Games, and Alibaba in the Greater Bay Area. The city is a center for gaming AI, mobile technology, and manufacturing robotics. OPPO and Vivo maintain major R&D centers in the broader Pearl River Delta region.

label: Biren Technology, Shanghai (R&D Tianjin)
sub: Biren Technology develops China's highest-performance domestic GPU accelerators (BR100) designed to compete with Nvidia A100 for AI training. Founded in 2019 with $253M in funding. Added to US Entity List in October 2022, cutting off access to TSMC manufacturing — now forced to use SMIC.

label: Cambricon AI Chip Company, Beijing
sub: Cambricon Technologies (寒武纪) — China's first publicly listed AI chip company, spun out of Chinese Academy of Sciences research. Develops NPU (Neural Processing Unit) chips for edge AI and data center applications. Formerly a chip supplier to Huawei before HiSilicon built its own AI IP.

label: Samsung Electronics HQ, Suwon
sub: Samsung is the world's largest memory chip maker and a key node in global semiconductor supply chains involving China. Samsung's China sales and fab operations create complex dependencies — Samsung sells billions in chips to Chinese smartphone makers while competing with YMTC in NAND flash.

label: SK Hynix HQ, Icheon
sub: SK Hynix is the world's second-largest DRAM maker and a major supplier of HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) used in Nvidia AI chips. SK Hynix's Wuxi DRAM fab in China is a major source of tension — the company received temporary waivers to continue operating its China facilities under US export controls.

label: Tokyo Electron HQ (Japan Export Controls)
sub: Tokyo Electron (TEL) is the world's second-largest semiconductor equipment maker and a critical supplier to SMIC and Chinese fabs. Under US pressure, Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) imposed export controls on 23 types of advanced chip-making equipment effective July 2023, significantly restricting TEL's sales to Chinese customers.

label: ASML Japan Service Center, Osaka
sub: ASML maintains service centers in Japan to support chip equipment in Korea, Japan, and Taiwan. As part of coordinated Western export control strategy, Japan banned the export of advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment matching ASML's DUV immersion tools, limiting what Japan-based suppliers can ship to Chinese fabs.

label: US Embassy Beijing (Entity List Target Zone)
sub: The US Embassy in Beijing serves as the front-line diplomatic interface for US technology sanctions against Chinese entities. Over 1,300 Chinese companies and institutions have been added to US export control lists since 2018, making China the country with the most entities on the US Entity List.

label: ByteDance Regional HQ, Hong Kong
sub: ByteDance maintains significant operations in Hong Kong as an intermediary between Chinese mainland and international markets. Hong Kong's changing political status post-2020 National Security Law has complicated ByteDance's use of HK as a buffer between its Chinese parent and US-regulated operations.

label: Alibaba Cloud Data Center, Guiyang
sub: Alibaba's massive Guiyang data center complex in Guizhou province is one of China's largest cloud computing facilities. Guizhou's cool climate, cheap hydroelectric power, and government support make it a hub for Chinese hyperscaler infrastructure. Baidu, Huawei Cloud, and government data systems also maintain major facilities here.

label: Hohhot AI Data Center Hub, Inner Mongolia
sub: Inner Mongolia hosts massive data center clusters for Baidu, China Telecom, and government AI training workloads, powered by cheap coal-generated electricity. As Chinese companies stockpile Nvidia chips before export controls take effect, Hohhot has seen rapid expansion of GPU cluster deployments for AI training.

label: Chongqing Smart Manufacturing Hub
sub: Chongqing is China's largest municipality and a major hub for automotive manufacturing, industrial robots, and electronics assembly. Home to major Xiaomi and Lenovo manufacturing operations. The city exemplifies Made in China 2025's vision of upgrading traditional manufacturing with AI, robotics, and digital twins.

label: Tianjin Binhai New Area Tech Zone
sub: Tianjin's Binhai New Area is a major hub for AI manufacturing applications, industrial robotics, and aerospace technology. Hosts China's National Supercomputer Center (Tianhe-1A) and major R&D facilities for aerospace and aviation technology.

label: Samsung HBM Research, Hwaseong
sub: Samsung and SK Hynix dominate HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) production — the critical component stacked atop Nvidia and AMD AI GPUs. China's inability to access HBM from Korean suppliers, given US controls on advanced memory exports, is a key bottleneck to China building competitive AI training clusters.

label: Huawei Pakistan 5G & Smart City, Lahore
sub: Huawei has been central to Pakistan's 5G rollout and Safe City surveillance projects, partnering with government agencies in Lahore, Islamabad, and Karachi. Pakistan's close strategic alignment with China and its refusal to follow US-led Huawei bans makes it a flagship market for Huawei's 'digital silk road' strategy. Huawei's training centers have developed 15,000+ Pakistani engineers.

label: China Tech Investment Hub, Almaty
sub: Kazakhstan's financial capital Almaty has become a hub for Chinese technology companies expanding westward along the Belt and Road. Alibaba Cloud, Huawei, and multiple Chinese fintech companies have established regional offices. As a Eurasian transit hub, Kazakhstan is central to China's digital silk road strategy connecting China to Europe via Central Asian tech infrastructure.

label: Huawei Philippines 5G Rollout, Manila
sub: Huawei supplies 5G network equipment to Philippine telcos PLDT and Globe, despite US pressure to ban Chinese vendors. The Philippines' geographic position at the intersection of US security alliances and China's tech influence makes it a key battleground in the 5G vendor selection contest. Huawei trained 20,000+ Filipino engineers through its ICT Academy program.

label: China Manufacturing Shift to Vietnam, Da Nang
sub: Vietnam has become the primary recipient of Chinese electronics manufacturing relocating due to US-China trade war tariffs. Samsung, Intel, LG, and dozens of Chinese suppliers have invested $60B+ in Vietnamese manufacturing. Da Nang and Hanoi represent the 'China+1' strategy where multinationals keep China production while building Vietnam alternatives — a partial but incomplete decoupling.

label: Huawei Siberia Enterprise Hub, Novosibirsk
sub: Russia's post-2022 isolation from Western tech accelerated adoption of Chinese technology alternatives. Huawei enterprise products, Lenovo servers, and Chinese software platforms have significantly expanded in Russian market. Novosibirsk, home to Russia's Akademgorodok tech hub, exemplifies deeper Sino-Russian tech cooperation that emerged as a counterweight to Western export controls on both countries.

label: Wuhan Optics Valley — Fiber Optics & Telecom
sub: Wuhan's East Lake High-Tech Zone (Optics Valley) is China's leading fiber optics and photonics manufacturing center. Yangtze Optical Fiber and Cable (YOFC), a Huawei affiliate, produces more fiber optic cable here than anywhere on Earth. The cluster also includes laser technology and integrated photonic chip research critical for next-gen communications.

label: Haier Smart Home IoT Center, Qingdao
sub: Haier's Qingdao headquarters is also home to COSMOPlat, China's largest industrial internet platform. Haier uses AI, IoT, and digital twins to connect 100+ factories, serving as a showcase for Made in China 2025's 'intelligent manufacturing' vision. COSMOPlat is being exported globally as China's industrial internet standard.

label: DeepSeek AI Lab, Hangzhou
sub: DeepSeek, the Chinese AI lab that shocked the world with its V3 and R1 models, is headquartered in Hangzhou. Founded by High-Flyer (幻方科技), a quantitative hedge fund, DeepSeek's R1 model achieved performance comparable to OpenAI's o1 at reportedly a fraction of the cost, triggering Nvidia's $600B market cap crash in January 2025.

label: ASML Taiwan Service Center, Hsinchu
sub: ASML's Taiwan service hub supports TSMC's fleet of EUV and DUV lithography machines — tools that China cannot legally purchase or receive service for under US-coordinated export controls. ASML's monopoly on EUV technology (essential for sub-7nm chips) is the single most critical chokepoint in China's semiconductor ambitions.

label: CAICT — China AI & Telecom Standards Lab
sub: The China Academy of Information and Communications Technology (CAICT) is China's primary telecom and AI standards body under MIIT. CAICT shapes China's 5G standards submissions to ITU and 3GPP, and publishes China's AI governance frameworks. Its standards diplomacy gives China disproportionate influence in global tech governance through multilateral bodies.

label: Tianjin Port — DeepSeek/Huawei AI Automation Hub
sub: Tianjin Port's Second Container Terminal achieved 88% process automation in March 2026 using Huawei's 'PortGPT' (Pangu model) integrated with DeepSeek's LLM — a flagship example of China's 15th Five-Year Plan 'AI+ action plan'. Operations efficiency improved 45%, crane utilization rose 12.3%. Tianjin Port is the world's 8th largest by throughput and a key gateway for China's northeastern industrial heartland.

label: Xiong'an New Area — National AI Demonstration Zone
sub: China's purpose-built satellite city 100km south of Beijing, designated in the 15th Five-Year Plan (2026–2030) as one of three national AI demonstration zones. The Xiong'an 'AI+' Industry Forum (April 2026) unveiled comprehensive R&D incentives and GPU cluster subsidies for AI enterprises. Xiong'an is a flagship showcase for China's AI-integrated urban development strategy, hosting autonomous vehicle testing corridors, smart grid infrastructure, and digital twin city management systems.

label: Zhipu AI Lab, Beijing
sub: Zhipu AI (智谱AI) is one of China's leading AI startups, spun out of Tsinghua University's KEG Lab. Developer of the GLM series of large language models and enterprise AI agents. Valued at approximately $14 billion (March 2026). Zhipu shares surged 35% in Hong Kong in March 2026 despite widening losses, reflecting investor confidence in its agentic AI commercialization strategy. CEO Zhang Peng cites Anthropic as a commercialization benchmark.

label: SEMICON China 2026, Shanghai New International Expo Centre
sub: SEMICON China 2026 (March 25–27) hosted 1,500 exhibitors and 5,000+ booths — the largest edition in the event's history. Highlighted China's semiconductor capacity expansion trajectory: domestic output share of 22–40nm chips to grow from 37% to 42% by 2028. The conference showcased domestic equipment, advanced packaging, and chiplet integration as the three pillars of China's self-sufficiency under the 15th Five-Year Plan.

label: Shenzhen 10,000-Card Huawei Ascend AI Cluster
sub: China's first 10,000-card fully domestic AI computing cluster, activated April 2026 using exclusively Huawei Ascend 910C chips. Delivers 14,000 petaflops total capacity (11,000 new + 3,000 existing) — equivalent to 5.5 million personal computers. Zero Nvidia hardware. 92% booking rate from 50+ organizations including DeepSeek (for V4 training). A flagship milestone in China's 15th Five-Year Plan AI compute infrastructure buildout and validation of domestic chip self-sufficiency.
